God commands Jeremiah to commit His warnings to a written scroll. The prophet cannot go to the temple himself—he is restricted, perhaps imprisoned or under threat—so his scribe Baruch becomes his voice. The strategy is clear and honest: read the words publicly during a fast day, and "perhaps" the people will repent and God will forgive their sin. There is no certainty here, only an opening door. God is not manipulating; He is genuinely offering a way back. The scroll is written. The words are spoken. And then the machinery of rejection begins to turn.

What happens next reveals how a society hardens itself against God's truth. The officials actually listen. They tremble. They understand the weight of what they've heard. Some even plead with the king not to burn the scroll. But King Jehoiakim responds differently. As Baruch reads the words to him in his winter quarters beside a warm fire, the king cuts the scroll into pieces and throws them into the flames. With each column consumed, he refuses to fear. His servants do the same. They have heard the exact same message as the officials, yet their hearts move toward destruction rather than repentance. The difference is not in the clarity of God's word—it is in the will to receive it.

But here is what Jehoiakim did not understand: you cannot burn the word of the Lord. God's response is immediate. He commands Jeremiah to write the scroll again, and to add to it—more words, similar words, a fuller condemnation. The king's defiance does not silence God; it compounds his judgment. Jehoiakim will have no heir on David's throne. His body will be cast out without burial, exposed to the elements. The calamity he rejected will come upon him and his people anyway. By rejecting God's written warning, he has not escaped judgment—he has sealed it.

The chapter ends with Baruch holding a new scroll, writing again what was lost. This is the persistence of God's word: it cannot be destroyed by fire, by power, by pride, or by the deliberate refusal to listen. The real question the chapter leaves with you is not whether God's word will accomplish its purpose—it will. The question is what you will do with it now that you have heard it. Will you be among those who tremble and turn, or among those who burn it in the fire of your own will?
